literal: Thereupon Yao tried Shun with the five norms and the hundred offices; all were governed.
idiomatic: Yao then tested Shun with the five relationships and every bureau—everything thrived.

literal: Formerly the Gaoyang clan had eight talented sons; the world gained their benefit and called them the Eight Harmonious.
idiomatic: The Gaoyang line once had eight brilliant sons whose gifts blessed the age—the “Eight Harmonious.”

literal: The Gaoxin clan had eight talented sons; the world called them the Eight Pures.
idiomatic: The Gaoxin line had eight more paragons whom men called the “Eight Pures.”

literal: These sixteen families generation after generation completed their excellence and did not let their names fall.
idiomatic: These sixteen houses handed down their virtue and never stained their fame.

literal: Down to Yao, Yao could not raise them.
idiomatic: Yet even under Yao they had not been promoted.

literal: Shun raised the Eight Harmonious and made them oversee the earth to measure the hundred tasks; nothing missed its season.
idiomatic: Shun raised the Eight Harmonious and set them over the land to pace the hundred labors—nothing fell out of season.

literal: He raised the Eight Pures and sent them to spread the five teachings through the four quarters: father righteous, mother kind, elder brother friendly, younger brother respectful, son filial; within there was peace, without there was completion.
idiomatic: He raised the Eight Pures to spread the five teachings abroad—fathers just, mothers tender, elder brothers kind, younger brothers deferential, sons filial—so that home was calm and the realm perfected.

literal: Formerly the Emperor Hong clan had an unworthy son who hid righteousness and harbored villainy, loving to do violence; the world called him Chaos.
idiomatic: Long ago the Hong clan bred a villain who smothered right and nursed evil, delighting in cruelty—men named him Chaos.

literal: The Shaohao clan had an unworthy son who destroyed trust and hated loyalty, glorifying evil speech; the world called him Wretch.
idiomatic: The Shaohao line had a rogue who broke faith and spurned loyalty, trumpeting slander—men called him Wretch.

literal: The Zhuanxu clan had an unworthy son who could not be taught or instructed, who did not understand good words; the world called him Blockhead.
idiomatic: The Zhuanxu clan produced a brute who would not learn or heed counsel—men called him Blockhead.

literal: These three families generation after generation were the world’s sorrow.
idiomatic: For generations these three houses were the scourge of the age.

literal: Down to Yao, Yao could not remove them.
idiomatic: Even Yao could not uproot them.

literal: The Jinyun clan had an unworthy son greedy in drink and food, greedy for bribes; the world called him Glutton.
idiomatic: The Jinyun clan added a glutton who gorged on food and wine and stuffed his purse—men called him Glutton.

literal: The world hated them and compared them to the three villains.
idiomatic: The people loathed him and classed him with the three fiends.

literal: Shun guested at the four gates and thereupon banished these four evil clans, removing them to the four edges to ward off demons and sprites; thus the four gates opened and none said there were evil men.
idiomatic: When Shun received the lords at the four gates he exiled the four wicked lines to the rim of the world to drive off demons—so the gates stood open and none dared call evil “minister.”

literal: Shun entered the great forest; fierce wind, thunder, and rain did not confuse him; Yao then knew Shun was fit to receive all under Heaven.
idiomatic: Shun walked the great moor in gales and thunder and never lost his way; Yao knew he could receive the mandate.

literal: Yao being aged, he made Shun act as regent for the Son of Heaven’s government and go on tour.
idiomatic: When Yao grew feeble he made Shun regent and sent him to tour the realm.

literal: Shun was raised to office twenty years, and Yao made him regent.
idiomatic: Twenty years Shun held office before Yao named him regent.

literal: Eight years he held the regency and Yao died.
idiomatic: Eight years as regent, then Yao died.

literal: When the three years’ mourning were finished he yielded to Dan Zhu, yet all under Heaven turned to Shun.
idiomatic: After mourning he yielded to Dan Zhu, but the realm rallied to Shun.

literal: Yu, Gao Yao, Qi, Hou Ji, Boyi, Kui, Long, Chui, Yi, Peng Zu had all been raised from Yao’s time but had not yet received separate duties.
idiomatic: Yu, Gao Yao, Qi, Hou Ji, Boyi, Kui, Long, Chui, Yi, and Peng Zu had served since Yao’s day without fixed portfolios.

literal: Thereupon Shun went to Wen Temple, consulted the Four Peaks, opened the four gates, cleared the eyes and ears of the four quarters, commanded the twelve pastors to discourse on Emperor Yao’s virtue, to carry deep kindness, to banish flatterers, and then the barbarians all submitted.
idiomatic: Shun entered Wenzu, spoke with the Four Peaks, opened the four gates, sharpened sight and hearing across the realm, ordered the twelve pastors to expound Yao’s virtue, spread deep kindness, drive off sycophants—and the wild tribes submitted.

literal: Shun said to the Four Peaks, “Who can vigorously accomplish Yao’s affairs and may be placed in office to assist?”
idiomatic: Shun asked the Four Peaks, “Who can carry on Yao’s work with zeal and serve as minister?”

literal: All said, “Lord Yu as Minister of Works can complete the emperor’s merit.”
idiomatic: All answered, “Lord Yu as Minister of Works can finish what Yao began.”

literal: Shun said, “Alas, yes!
idiomatic: Shun said, “So be it!

literal: Yu, you level the waters and earth—only strive at this.”
idiomatic: Yu—you must tame the flood; bend every effort to it.”

literal: Yu bowed his head to the ground, yielding to Ji, Qi, and Gao Yao.
idiomatic: Yu kowtowed and tried to yield to Ji, Qi, and Gao Yao.

literal: Shun said, “Yes, go.”
idiomatic: Shun said, “Go—you are the man.”

literal: Shun said, “Qi, the black-haired people are beginning to starve; you, Hou Ji, sow in season the hundred grains.”
idiomatic: Shun told Hou Ji, “The black-haired people are beginning to hunger—sow the hundred grains in their seasons.”

literal: Shun said, “Qi, the hundred surnames are not intimate, the five grades are not tame; you be Minister of Education and reverently spread the five teachings; be lenient.”
idiomatic: Shun told Qi, “Kinship falters and the five relations waver—be Minister of Education, spread the five teachings with gentle patience.”

literal: Shun said, “Gao Yao, the outer barbarians disturb the Xia, bandits, thieves, and traitors arise; you be Minister of Crime—the five punishments have their applications, the five applications have three venues.
idiomatic: Shun told Gao Yao, “Barbarians harass the heartland and villains breed—be Minister of Crime; let the five punishments fit the crime, with three degrees of enforcement.

literal: The five exiles have their measures, the three measures have three dwellings: only enlightenment can keep faith.”
idiomatic: Exile has five grades and three dwelling zones—only a clear judge wins trust.”

literal: Shun said, “Who can tame my workmen?”
idiomatic: Shun asked, “Who will master my artisans?”

literal: All said Chui might.
idiomatic: All named Chui.

literal: Thereupon he made Chui Minister of Works.
idiomatic: Shun made Chui Minister of Works.

literal: Shun said, “Who can tame for me above and below the grasses and trees, birds and beasts?”
idiomatic: He asked next, “Who will oversee forests, marshes, birds, and beasts?”

literal: All said Yi might.
idiomatic: All named Yi.

literal: Thereupon he made Yi Director of the Forbidden Parks.
idiomatic: He appointed Yi master of the royal preserves.

literal: Yi bowed his head to the ground, yielding to the ministers Zhu Hu and Xiongpi.
idiomatic: Yi kowtowed and tried to yield to Zhu Hu and Xiongpi.

literal: Shun said, “Go—you harmonize them.”
idiomatic: Shun said, “Go—you shall work with them.”

literal: Thereupon he used Zhu Hu and Xiongpi as assistants.
idiomatic: He named Zhu Hu and Xiongpi as assistants.

literal: Shun said, “Alas!
idiomatic: Shun cried, “Hear me!

literal: You Four Peaks—is there one who can preside over my three rites?”
idiomatic: O Four Peaks—who will superintend the three great rites?”

literal: All said Boyi might.
idiomatic: All named Boyi.

literal: Shun said, “Alas!
idiomatic: Shun said, “Then,

literal: Boyi, I make you Director of the Ancestral Order; morning and night be reverent; be straight, be still, be pure.”
idiomatic: Boyi, you shall be Director of the Ancestral Order—watch day and night, upright, calm, and stainless.”

literal: Boyi yielded to Kui and Long.
idiomatic: Boyi tried to yield to Kui and Long.

literal: Shun said, “Yes.
idiomatic: Shun said, “Very well.

literal: I make Kui Director of Music to teach the noble sons—straight yet warm, wide yet firm, hard without cruelty, simple without arrogance.
idiomatic: Kui shall be Director of Music to train the heirs—straight yet kind, broad yet disciplined, stern without cruelty, plain without arrogance.

literal: Poetry speaks the intent, song prolongs the speech, notes rely on the prolongation, pitchpipes harmonize the notes; when the eight kinds of sound harmonize and none seize one another’s order, spirits and men are at peace.”
idiomatic: Poetry voices the mind, song draws out the words, melody follows the drawn tones, pitch rules the melody—when the eight timbres blend without usurping one another, gods and men are reconciled.”

literal: Kui said, “Oh!
idiomatic: Kui answered, “Ah!

literal: I strike the stone and pat the stone and the hundred beasts lead the dance.”
idiomatic: When I strike and tap the sounding-stone, the hundred beasts all fall into step.”

literal: Shun said, “Long, I fear slanderous words and treacherous deeds that alarm my hosts; I command you to be the mouth of reception—morning and night issue and receive my commands—only trustworthiness.”
idiomatic: Shun told Long, “Slander and deceit would shake my people—you shall be chief of reception; issue and record my orders day and night with perfect good faith.”

literal: Shun said, “Alas!
idiomatic: Shun said, “You

literal: you twenty-two men, be reverent—each in his season assist Heaven’s affairs.”
idiomatic: twenty-two—be vigilant; each in season help heaven’s work.”

literal: Every three years they examined merit; after three examinations demotions and promotions were settled, and near and far all tasks flourished.
idiomatic: Every third year he reviewed their deeds; after three reviews the unworthy fell and the worthy rose, and every task near or far throve.

literal: He divided and sent north the Three Miao.
idiomatic: He broke the power of the Three Miao and drove them north.

literal: These twenty-two men all completed their merit: Gao Yao as chief judge was fair and the people each submitted to the truth.
idiomatic: Those twenty-two all proved their worth: Gao Yao as judge was even-handed and the people trusted his verdicts.

literal: Boyi presided over the rites and above and below showed yielding courtesy.
idiomatic: Boyi kept the rites and high and low deferred with grace.

literal: Chui as master of workmen made the hundred crafts achieve their work.
idiomatic: Chui as minister of works made every craft succeed.

literal: Yi as park director opened hills and marshes.
idiomatic: Yi as warden opened hills and marshes.

literal: Qi as lord of grain made the hundred grains flourish in season.
idiomatic: Qi as lord of grain made every crop thrive in its season.

literal: Qi as Minister of Education made the hundred surnames intimate and harmonious.
idiomatic: Qi as minister of education knit the clans in harmony.

literal: Long as guest director brought distant men to court.
idiomatic: Long as master of guests drew far peoples in.

literal: The twelve pastors traveled and within the nine provinces none dared to shirk or rebel.
idiomatic: The twelve pastors rode circuit and none in the nine provinces dared defy them.

literal: Only Yu’s merit was greatest: he opened the nine mountains, linked the nine marshes, dredged the nine rivers, fixed the nine provinces; each region offered tribute according to its office and never missed what was proper.
idiomatic: Yet Yu’s work towered above the rest—he cut passes through nine ranges, channeled nine swamps, dredged nine rivers, and settled nine provinces, each paying tribute in kind without fault.

literal: Five thousand li formed a square to the wild border.
idiomatic: His domain ran five thousand li square to the wild frontier.

literal: South he soothed Jiaozhi, Beifa, western Rong, Zhizhi, Qusou, Di, and Qiang; north Mount Rong, Fa, and Sushen; east Chang and the bird-barbarians: within the four seas they all bore the virtue of Emperor Shun.
idiomatic: South to Jiaozhi and Beifa, west to the Rong tribes, north to Mount Rong, Fa, and Sushen, east to the long coast and bird Yi—all within the seas praised Shun’s virtue.

literal: Thereupon Yu raised the music of the Nine Summons; strange creatures came; phoenixes came and circled.
idiomatic: Yu then performed the Nine Summons; prodigies appeared and phoenixes wheeled overhead.

literal: All under Heaven’s bright virtue began from the Yu Emperor (Shun).
idiomatic: The luminous virtue that spread through the realm began with Emperor Shun of Yu.

literal: Shun at twenty was famed for filial piety; at thirty Yao raised him; at fifty he acted as regent; at fifty-eight Yao died; at sixty-one he replaced Yao and mounted the imperial seat.
idiomatic: Famed for filial piety at twenty, chosen at thirty, regent at fifty, he mourned Yao’s death at fifty-eight, and at sixty-one inherited the throne.

literal: Thirty-nine years on the throne he toured south and died in the wilds of Cangwu.
idiomatic: Thirty-nine years he reigned, then died on his southern tour in the wilds of Cangwu.

literal: He was buried south of the Yangzi at Jiuyi; this is called Lingling.
idiomatic: He was buried south of the river at Jiuyi—what men call Lingling.

literal: When Shun mounted the throne he flew the Son of Heaven’s banners and went to court on his father Gu Sou, trembling in utmost reverence as a son’s way requires.
idiomatic: On taking the throne he rode with imperial pennons to visit blind Gu Sou, trembling with the awe a son owes a father.

literal: He enfeoffed his younger brother Xiang as a feudal lord.
idiomatic: He ennobled his brother Xiang as a lord.

literal: Shun’s son Shangjun was also not worthy; Shun therefore beforehand commended Yu to Heaven.
idiomatic: His son Shangjun proved unworthy, so Shun long before had commended Yu to heaven.

literal: Seventeen years and he died.
idiomatic: Seventeen years he reigned after that, then died.

literal: When the three years’ mourning were finished, Yu likewise yielded to Shun’s son as Shun had yielded to Yao’s son.
idiomatic: After mourning Yu yielded to Shun’s son as Shun had once yielded to Yao’s son.

literal: The feudal lords turned to him, and afterward Yu mounted the Son of Heaven’s seat.
idiomatic: The lords rallied to Yu, and he then took the throne.

literal: Yao’s son Dan Zhu and Shun’s son Shangjun each had territory allotted to sacrifice to their ancestors.
idiomatic: Dan Zhu and Shangjun kept fiefs to tend their fathers’ shrines.

literal: They wore their fathers’ robes and used their music and rites.
idiomatic: They wore their fathers’ vestments and kept their fathers’ music and rites.

literal: As guests they were received by the Son of Heaven, who did not treat them as subjects, showing he dared not monopolize the realm.
idiomatic: The reigning king received them as guests, not vassals, to show the throne was never a private possession.

literal: From the Yellow Emperor to Shun and Yu all shared the same surname but changed their state names to display their bright virtue.
idiomatic: From Huangdi through Shun and Yu the line shared one surname yet changed dynastic titles to mark their shining virtue.

literal: Therefore the Yellow Emperor was Youxiong, Emperor Zhuanxu was Gaoyang, Emperor Ku was Gaoxin, Emperor Yao was Taotang, Emperor Shun was Youyu.
idiomatic: Thus Huangdi styled his house Youxiong, Zhuanxu Gaoyang, Ku Gaoxin, Yao Taotang, Shun Youyu.

literal: Emperor Yu was lord of Xia and took a separate surname, the Si clan.
idiomatic: Emperor Yu founded Xia and took the clan name Si.

literal: Qi was lord of Shang, surname Zi.
idiomatic: Qi founded Shang with the clan name Zi.

literal: Qi was lord of Zhou, surname Ji.
idiomatic: Qi founded Zhou with the clan name Ji.

literal: The Grand Historian says: Scholars for the most part praise the Five Emperors—this is ancient indeed.
idiomatic: The Grand Historian remarks: Learned men praise the Five Emperors—yet their age lies far in the past.

literal: Yet the Document only records what comes from Yao onward.
idiomatic: The Classic of Documents begins only with Yao.

literal: The hundred schools speak of the Yellow Emperor, but their texts are not elegant or trustworthy, and gentry scholars hesitate to repeat them.
idiomatic: The hundred philosophers tell of the Yellow Emperor, yet their language is coarse and doubtful, which gentlemen seldom repeat.

literal: What Confucius transmitted—Zai Wo’s questions on the Virtues of the Five Emperors and the Genealogies and Surnames—some Confucians do not pass on.
idiomatic: Confucius handed down Zai Wo’s “Virtues of the Five Emperors” and the “Imperial Genealogies,” yet many Ruists decline to teach them.

literal: I have traveled west to Hollow Tong, north past Zhuolu, east to the sea, south along the Yangzi and Huai; elders in each place often pointed out the haunts of the Yellow Emperor, Yao, and Shun, yet their customs and teachings differed; in sum, what does not stray from the ancient texts is near the truth.
idiomatic: I have ridden west to Hollow Tong, north beyond Zhuolu, east to the sea, and south along the Yangzi and Huai; everywhere elders still pointed out where Huangdi, Yao, and Shun dwelt, though local lore varied—yet what agrees with the old documents is likeliest true.

literal: I examined the Spring and Autumn Annals and the Discourses of the States; they develop the Virtues of the Five Emperors and the Genealogies and Surnames quite clearly, though scholars have not dug deeply—their testimony is not empty.
idiomatic: Reading the Spring and Autumn Annals and Discourses of the States, I find their accounts of the Five Emperors’ virtue and the royal genealogies lucid, though scholars rarely study them in depth; what they record is not idle tale.

literal: The Documents have gaps; their scattered fragments appear in other traditions.
idiomatic: The Documents have lacunae; stray passages surface in other works.

literal: Unless one loves learning and thinks deeply to grasp their meaning, it is hard to explain them to shallow views and narrow hearing.
idiomatic: Without fond learning and deep thought to seize their sense, one cannot expound them for the shallow-minded.

literal: I have arranged and discussed them, choosing the most elegant language, therefore I set them at the head of the basic annals.
idiomatic: I have ordered and selected the most polished accounts, and therefore place them at the opening of the annals.
